title: TPU v1 · Wafer Fabrication cycle time and capacity: after a wafer lot starts, it must traverse hundreds of fab operations over weeks or months before completed wafers emerge. The long cycle is barely compressible, and each layer makes the process effectively irreversible—a critical-layer error can scrap the lot and force a restart. Capacity is further constrained by foundry scheduling and 28 nm node maturity. This long interval governs supply and launch timing, consumes a substantial part of TPU’s “approximately 15 months from project start to deployment,” and leaves little tolerance for repeated iteration
oneLine: This case focuses on “Wafer Fabrication cycle time and capacity.” Manufacturing is not a push-button operation. After a wafer lot starts, it queues through hundreds of deposition, lithography, etch, implantation, CMP, and other operations in a cleanroom line, building the die layer by layer. This cycle time is measured in weeks or months and is nearly impossible to compress. More critically, it is highly irreversible: once materials have been successively deposited, etched, and doped, a mistake in any critical operation cannot simply be undone; the lot may have to be scrapped and restarted, incurring another full cycle. Capacity faces two principal constraints: the foundry’s production schedule and the maturity and work-in-process (WIP) ceiling of the 28 nm node. Together they determine how soon usable TPUs arrive and how broadly supply can scale. Connecting this to TPU itself, the Jouppi team went from project start to datacenter deployment in approximately 15 months, with tapeout scheduling and manufacturing consuming a substantial share. Because the cycle is long and irreversible, the path cannot sustain repeated rework. This section uses TPU’s known 256×256 systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz clock, 28 nm process, ≤331 mm² die, and PCIe accelerator card only as the object moving through this long production cycle. Specific cycle days, WIP counts, capacity, and schedules follow standard foundry practice for this class of 28 nm large chip; TPU v1 does not disclose its foundry or proprietary manufacturing data.

legend.name: Fab production flow (hundreds of sequential operations before completion)
legend.desc: The main body of cycle time. A wafer must sequentially pass through hundreds of deposition → lithography → etch → ion implantation → CMP and other operations, building transistors and a dozen-plus metal layers. Between operations, it also queues for different tools. TPU’s 256×256 systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, accumulators, and interconnect do not appear in a single step; they take shape layer by layer through this sequence. The operation count alone stretches fabrication into weeks or months, which is why cycle time cannot be compressed simply by “adding people or money.” Exact operation counts, equipment, and queue times are standard proprietary details of a 28 nm foundry process and are not disclosed for TPU.

legend.name: Cycle time (weeks to months, barely compressible)
legend.desc: The most counterintuitive manufacturing property: from wafer-lot start at T0 to completed wafers, the cycle takes weeks or months and cannot be shortened substantially. It is determined by serial completion of hundreds of physical/chemical operations, each operation’s intrinsic duration, and equipment queues—not by compute throughput. Additional budget cannot make an etch or anneal finish instantly. The implication is stark: wafers started today reveal their quality only weeks or months later, and any design or process change requires another full cycle for validation. For a schedule such as TPU’s approximately 15-month deployment, this is one of the hardest, least avoidable blocks of time. Specific cycle days are foundry-proprietary; the discussion is illustrative of standard practice.

legend.name: Irreversibility (critical-layer error = scrap and restart the lot)
legend.desc: Long cycle time becomes especially costly when combined with irreversibility. Once materials have been successively deposited, etched, and doped on the wafer, there is no general “undo and redo.” If a critical operation—overlay, defect excursion, or recipe drift—fails, the work-in-process lot may need to be scrapped, restarted, and sent through another complete cycle. Manufacturing experiments are therefore expensive: unlike changing and recompiling a line of software, one error can cost weeks or months and an entire wafer lot. This is why upstream tapeout signoff in ⑩ and process monitoring must be exceptionally rigorous; errors should be blocked before wafer start. Irreversibility is a general manufacturing fact; TPU’s specific rework and scrap data are not public.

legend.name: Capacity (constrained by foundry scheduling + 28 nm node maturity)
legend.desc: How many devices arrive, and how quickly, depends on capacity as well as cycle time. First is foundry scheduling: fab capacity is a scarce shared resource, and a customer’s lots must secure slots also sought by others. Second is maturity of the 28 nm node: mature nodes offer steadier yield and more predictable capacity, while new or constrained nodes are limited. Third is the work-in-process (WIP) ceiling: the line can hold only a finite number of simultaneous lots. Together these factors determine supply scale and launch/ramp speed. For TPU deployment at datacenter scale, capacity directly controls how many accelerator cards can be produced. Exact schedules, node capacity, and WIP limits are proprietary to foundries and the industry; this discussion illustrates standard practice.

legend.name: Foundry schedule (production slots + node maturity)
legend.desc: A concrete source of capacity constraint. A foundry shares production resources among many customers, so when a lot can start and how many wafers it receives depend on available slots and the maturity and tightness of its process node. A delayed slot compounds a manufacturing cycle already measured in weeks or months, pushing out the entire interval from design freeze to usable silicon. Chip projects must therefore reserve foundry capacity early and include tapeout and manufacturing schedules in the master plan. For a compressed program such as TPU’s approximately 15-month deployment, allocation delay is a real schedule variable. Exact scheduling and capacity allocation are foundry-proprietary; this discussion illustrates standard practice.

steps.cap: ① Start the lot and the clock: a wafer lot enters the fab and cycle timing begins at T0. It queues through hundreds of deposition, lithography, etch, implantation, CMP, and other operations, progressively building the 256×256 array and interconnect—this flow is the body of cycle time

② Complete hundreds of operations over weeks or months before wafers emerge: serial operations + intrinsic durations + tool queues set a cycle that is barely compressible. Only after completed wafers reach wafer-level electrical test does the lot’s quality become known

③ Long cycle + irreversibility: once materials have been built up layer by layer, there is no general “undo and redo.” A critical-operation error can scrap the entire lot, forcing a restart and another full cycle. Experimentation is extremely expensive, so errors must be stopped before wafer start during ⑩ signoff

④ Capacity is constrained by allocation and node conditions: foundry production slots + 28 nm node maturity + the work-in-process (WIP) ceiling jointly determine how soon and how many usable TPUs arrive, directly affecting supply, launch, and ramp timing

⑤ Cycle × allocation consumes project time: manufacturing cycle time plus tapeout/fab scheduling takes a substantial portion of TPU’s “approximately 15 months from project start to deployment.” Long cycles and irreversibility leave little tolerance for repeated rework

before: Reserve foundry capacity and production slots first: Manufacturing depends heavily on shared fab capacity. Before wafer start, reserve production slots early and agree on lot volume and delivery windows. Capacity is a scarce resource allocated among competing customers; without a slot, even an excellent design simply waits. Exact schedules and allocations are foundry-proprietary; this is standard practice.

Include fabrication cycle time in the master schedule: Project milestones must treat the weeks-to-months manufacturing cycle time and tapeout scheduling as hard constraints. This interval is barely compressible and forms the most rigid block between design freeze and available silicon. For a compressed schedule such as TPU’s approximately 15-month deployment, it requires sufficient contingency.

Confirm node maturity matches target volume: For the relatively mature 28 nm node, verify yield stability and releasable capacity, then estimate how many wafer lots and cycles are required to meet target volume. A more mature node makes capacity and cycle performance more predictable. TPU’s actual node capacity and yield are not public; this is a standard-practice illustration.

Include “irreversibility + long cycle” in the risk plan: Because successively formed layers generally cannot be reworked and one mistake can consume an entire cycle, establish extremely strict design signoff in ⑩, process monitoring, and critical-layer inspection before wafer start. Also reserve time and wafer capacity for the possibility of a scrapped lot and restart. Errors must be prevented before the start, not assumed fixable afterward.

Plan WIP and wafer-start cadence: The production line can accommodate only a finite number of lots at once. Plan staggered starts and WIP levels to balance early silicon for validation against limiting exposure to lot-wide scrap. This determines how smoothly capacity is released and how steadily supply scales.
during: Fundamental rule: fabrication cycle time is measured in weeks or months and is barely compressible. From wafer-lot start to completed wafers, hundreds of physical and chemical operations must run serially, each with intrinsic duration and intervening equipment queues. This is not a compute problem and cannot be eliminated by adding people or money. Understanding a manufacturing schedule begins with this hard floor: wafers started today reveal their quality only weeks or months later.

Long cycle × irreversibility = extremely expensive iteration. Once materials have been successively deposited, etched, and doped, there is no general “undo and redo.” A critical-operation error can scrap the WIP lot, forcing another wafer start and a complete additional cycle. Unlike changing and recompiling software, one manufacturing mistake consumes weeks or months plus an entire wafer lot. This is why tapeout signoff in ⑩ and process monitoring must be exhaustive, blocking errors before wafer start.

Capacity faces three constraints: foundry scheduling + node maturity + WIP. How soon and how many usable TPUs arrive depends on more than single-lot cycle time. It also depends on whether the fab allocates production slots, whether capacity at the 28 nm node is available and stable, and how many active lots the line can contain simultaneously. Together these determine supply scale and ramp speed. The line is a shared scarce resource, so each customer competes for slots.

Cycle time + scheduling directly set launch and supply cadence. Manufacturing cycle time compounded by tapeout and production scheduling pushes out the entire span from design freeze to usable silicon. At deployment scale—for example, installing many accelerator cards in datacenters—how far and how quickly supply can expand is largely determined by this cycle and available capacity. The interval cannot be bypassed; capacity must be reserved early and sufficient time budgeted.

Returning to TPU: this long interval consumed a substantial part of the “approximately 15 months to deployment”. The Jouppi team moved from project start to datacenter deployment in approximately 15 months, and TPU had been operating in datacenters for more than a year before public announcement. Manufacturing and tapeout scheduling consumed a meaningful share. Long cycle time and irreversibility left little room for repeated rework: every additional restart would cost another complete manufacturing cycle and threaten the compressed schedule.

Disclosure of process basis (applies throughout): Cycle times of weeks to months, manufacturing irreversibility, capacity constraints from foundry scheduling and node maturity, and work-in-process (WIP) ceilings are standard manufacturing and volume-production practices for this class of 28 nm large chip/PCIe accelerator. TPU v1 does not disclose its foundry, actual manufacturing-cycle days, WIP counts, capacity allocation, schedule, or yield. This section uses only TPU’s known 256×256 systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz clock, 28 nm process, ≤331 mm² die, and PCIe accelerator as the object being manufactured to explain cycle and capacity organization. Timelines, tick marks, and production slots in the figure are illustrative rather than proprietary TPU data; “approximately 15 months to deployment and operation for more than a year before publication” reflects the TPU project timing disclosed in the ISCA 2017 paper.

result.label: Manufacturing cycle time
result.value: A wafer lot passes through hundreds of operations over weeks or months from start to completed wafers; serial work and queues make the cycle barely compressible (specific days are foundry-proprietary; this reflects standard practice)

result.label: Irreversibility
result.value: Once materials are successively built up, there is no general undo; a critical-operation error can scrap the lot, forcing a new start and another complete cycle—making iteration extremely expensive

result.label: Capacity constraints
result.value: Foundry production scheduling + 28 nm node maturity + the work-in-process (WIP) ceiling jointly determine how soon and how many usable TPUs arrive

result.label: Impact on supply/launch
result.value: Cycle time + allocation push out the span from design freeze to silicon and directly govern supply scale and expansion/launch cadence; large accelerator deployments require early capacity reservations

result.label: Connection to TPU schedule
result.value: Manufacturing + tapeout scheduling consumed a substantial portion of the “approximately 15 months from project start to deployment.” Long cycle time + irreversibility made repeated rework untenable (project timing disclosed at ISCA 2017)

result.label: Disclosure boundary
result.value: TPU v1 does not disclose its foundry, actual cycle days, WIP counts, capacity/scheduling/yield; the descriptions above reflect standard foundry manufacturing and volume-production practice and illustrative values, not proprietary TPU data

after: Perform wafer sort/CP as soon as completed wafers emerge: After a lot completes its cycle, probe and power each die while still on the wafer. Only then is the lot’s quality known for the first time. Dies failing functional or electrical requirements are marked before dicing and packaging. This is the prelude to packaging/test in ⑫; wafer-level testing is standard practice.

Measure actual cycle/output and review the schedule: Summarize the lot’s realized cycle and good-die output, compare them with the plan, and assess whether foundry allocation and node capacity were delivered as expected. Use the findings to revise subsequent wafer-start cadence and capacity reservations. Cycle and capacity data are the basis of production planning. TPU’s actual cycle and output are not public; this is standard planning practice.

If a change is required, accept another complete cycle: A design or process correction requires a new wafer start and another weeks-to-months wait for validation because fabrication is irreversible and its cycle is not compressible. This forces teams to batch changes and maximize first-pass correctness rather than repeatedly start wafers for small increments—the practical meaning of “little tolerance for repeated rework.”

Feed cycle and capacity back into the master plan: Update the project schedule with actual fabrication cycle time and allocation delay, then re-estimate remaining time from design to deployment. For a compressed program such as TPU’s approximately 15-month deployment, this determines whether to reserve additional capacity or run multiple lots in parallel to protect the schedule. Detailed plans are project-proprietary; the timing disclosure appears in ISCA 2017.

Preserve an auditable record: Archive the lot’s start time, cycle, schedule, output, and electrical-test results in manufacturing/volume-production records for later fabrication, capacity-planning, and yield-analysis review. These records provide traceable manufacturing and delivery evidence for the dies; specific values remain proprietary to the foundry/project and are not public TPU disclosures.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper discloses TPU v1’s known 256×256 8-bit MAC systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 700 MHz clock, 28 nm process, ≤331 mm² die, and PCIe accelerator-card form factor, used here as the manufactured object; it also reports “approximately 15 months from project start to deployment” and operation in datacenters for more than a year before public announcement. A manufacturing cycle time of weeks to months, manufacturing irreversibility, foundry production scheduling, capacity constraints from 28 nm node maturity, WIP ceilings, and wafer-level electrical test are standard foundry/industry manufacturing and volume-production practices for this class of 28 nm large chip/PCIe accelerator. Timelines, tick marks, production slots, per-lot cycles, capacity, and yield in the figure are illustrative and are not public disclosures of TPU v1’s foundry, proprietary manufacturing-cycle days, WIP counts, capacity allocation, or yield)
